ness? 28-Horoscopes

MARCH 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 3

Algorithmic Emotions EDITORIAL Why social media Editor-in-Chief
MAGAZINE
Dr. Kamran Zuberi leaves us restless Social & Lifestyle Editor
Dr. Uzma Naaz
hen was the last time you Art & Culture Editor checked your phone without Dr. Monika Garg Wthinking about it? For many of us, the answer is uncomfortable. A quick Digital Strategy Editor glance becomes ten minutes. One notifica- Vishakha Sharma tion leads to another. Before we know it, we’ve slipped into a quiet ritual of scroll- ing, liking and comparing. Social Media Editor Platforms like Meta Platforms and Tik- Sushil Kumar Tok are designed to feel effortless. Swipe up, watch, repeat. The experience is Art & Graphics Director smooth, even pleasurable. But beneath that Vipin Agarwal simplicity lies a carefully engineered sys- tem that keeps us coming back for tiny bursts of satisfaction, the kind psychologists often describe as “digital dopamine.” EDITORIAL ADVISORY BOARD At first glance, social media promises connection. Friends across continents. Old classmates resurfacing. New ideas arriving every minute. Yet something curious D. Bisht Shandilya (Former News happens along the way. The same platforms that connect us also quietly cultivate Editor, United News of India, New comparison. Someone’s vacation photo. Another person’s promotion. A perfectly ed- ited moment that suggests life elsewhere is always brighter, richer, happier. Delhi), Prof. Amar Garg (Former It’s not just envy. It’s something subtler. Algorithms learn what excites us, what Vice Chancellor), Dr. Dharamveer annoys us, what keeps our eyes on the screen. The result is a kind of emotional roller Mahajan (Former Principal), Prof. coaster. One moment you’re laughing at a meme. The next, you’re reading alarming Archana Sharma (Pol. Science), news or watching an influencer’s flawless life. These algorithmic mood swings can Prof. P.D. Sharma (Botanist), Shahid leave users oddly restless, even after hours of scrolling. A Siddiqui IRS (Retd.) The paradox of hyper-connectivity is that people have never been more digitally linked, yet loneliness remains stubbornly high. Studies across several countries sug- gest that heavy social media use often correlates with increased anxiety and lower SUBSCRIPTIONS self-esteem, particularly among younger users. The question then becomes difficult to ignore. Are we choosing these platforms freely, or are we being subtly nudged to- ward dissatisfaction? Phone +91 20 40199102 Of course, social media is not purely villainous. Activism, creativity and com- Contact us Survey Number 12/1, munity have flourished online. For many young creators, these platforms provide op- Baner Road, portunity and voice. The problem is less about technology itself and more about how Pune-411045 (Maharashtra) it quietly shapes our attention and expectations. Email mail@magazine.revoir.in Perhaps the real challenge is learning to step back. To notice when the scroll stops Website www.revoir.in serving us. To remember that happiness rarely arrives through an algorithm. Maybe the simplest test is this. Put the phone down for a while and look around. Legal Advisor The world offline still moves at a human pace. And sometimes, that’s exactly the Anil Bakhshi (Advocate) speed happiness needs. And perhaps happiness, in the end, isn’t something to chase through endless feeds. It’s something quieter, found in moments that no algorithm can curate. Copyright © 2026 REVOIR (eZine), all rights reserved. This publication may not be reproduced in any form without permission.
Khzuberil Dr. Kamran Zuberi Editor in Chief zuberi@magazine.revoir.in
4 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MARCH 16 2026

COVER STORY
Digital Dopamine ness has become strangely entangled In today's attention economy, happi- with notifications, likes and endless scrolling. Social media promises con- Is social media nection and entertainment, yet many users walk away feeling restless or vaguely dissatisfied. The question feels uncomfortable but unavoidable: are these platforms simply tools we use, or stealing our systems quietly shaping how we feel?
The science of the digital reward loop At the heart of social media lies a happiness? powerful psychological trigger: dopa- mine. Dopamine is often described as the brain's “feel-good” chemical, though neuroscientists say it's really a moti- The deeper challenge may not be abandoning social vator. It pushes us to seek rewards. In healthy doses, it fuels curiosity, learning media but learning to use it without surrendering and pleasure. But when that reward sys- tem is repeatedly stimulated in unpre- emotional autonomy. By Naina Kapoor dictable ways, it can become habit- forming. Social media platforms tap directly t begins almost unconsciously. You inadequate. Ten minutes later, you're into this dynamic. wake up, reach for your phone and still there, chasing something you can't Every notification, like, comment or Istart scrolling. A friend's vacation quite name. share acts like a tiny reward. Sometimes photos. A stranger's fitness transfor- Maybe it's connection. Maybe dis- a post receives dozens of reactions. mation. A video that makes you laugh. traction. Or maybe it's something closer Sometimes none at all. That unpredict- Another that leaves you feeling oddly to a chemical reward. ability is crucial. It mirrors the mechan-
MARCH 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 5

“
In the endless scroll of curated
happiness, ordinary
life can begin to feel strangely
inadequate.”
becomes familiar. Someone is always fitter. Someone richer. Someone appears happier, more successful or more pro- ductive. Of course, these images rarely show the full story. Most posts represent high- light reels rather than ordinary life. But emotionally, the brain often forgets that distinction. Young adults and millennials are especially vulnerable to this comparison culture. For many, social media isn't just entertainment. It's tied to identity, friendships and career ambitions. A travel influencer's sunset photo might quietly trigger doubts about one's rou- tine life. A viral success story can create subtle pressure to achieve more, faster. None of this is deliberate cruelty. Yet ics used in casinos and slot machines. lored to individual tastes. the cumulative effect can be exhausting. Your brain keeps checking because the The experience feels magical. Some- Inspiration motivates us in small next reward might arrive at any moment. times unsettling. doses. In constant streams, it can make Apps run by companies such as Meta One moment you see uplifting travel ordinary life feel insufficient. Platforms and TikTok refine this loop videos. The next moment you're watch- constantly. Engineers analyze vast ing clips about burnout, loneliness or fi- The paradox of hyper-connectivity amounts of user data to determine what nancial stress. The algorithm isn't delib- Social media promised to bring the keeps people engaged the longest. The erately trying to make anyone unhappy. world closer together. In many ways, it result is a personalized stream of content It simply prioritizes engagement. And has succeeded. Families separated by that feels almost impossible to leave. emotionally charged content tends to continents share everyday moments in- And once that loop begins, it rarely keep people watching longer. stantly. Activists mobilize communities ends with a single glance. Some researchers now refer to this within hours. Friendships survive across phenomenon as algorithmic mood time zones. Algorithms that know our moods swings. Your emotional state can shift Yet something strange has happened If early social media felt like brow- depending on what the feed decides to along the way. sing a newspaper, today's platforms be- show next. It's a subtle influence, but a Despite being more digitally con- have more like mood-reading machines. powerful one. nected than ever, many users report feel- Algorithms track how long we watch ing lonelier offline. a video, whether we pause on a photo, The comparison trap Part of the paradox lies in how inter- what posts make us react emotionally. Humans have always compared actions have changed. Online communi- Over time they build detailed profiles of themselves with others. Social media cation often replaces deeper conver- our interests, habits and even our vulner- has simply multiplied the scale. sations rather than adding to them. abilities. Earlier generations compared them- Quick reactions, emojis and short com- TikTok's recommendation engine is selves with neighbors or colleagues. ments substitute for more meaningful often described as one of the most so- Today we measure our lives against in- dialogue. phisticated in the digital world. Within fluencers, celebrities and carefully cu- There is also what sociologists call minutes of opening the app, the “For rated lifestyles from across the globe. ambient intimacy. We know what ac- You” page begins delivering content tai- Scroll long enough and the pattern quaintances ate for breakfast, where col-
6 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MARCH 16 2026

tal reward loop replaces more grounded experiences. Researchers increasingly recommend small behavioral adjustments rather than extreme digital detoxes. Turning off non-essential notifications reduces im- pulsive checking. Limiting scrolling be- fore bedtime improves sleep. Even short breaks from social feeds can reset emo- tional perspective. Some users curate their timelines carefully, unfollowing accounts that trigger comparison or anxiety. Others practice mindful scrolling, asking a sim- ple question before opening an app: What am I here for? It's a small act of awareness, but surprisingly powerful.
Learning to reclaim our attention The deeper challenge may not be abandoning social media but learning to use it without surrendering emotional autonomy. Attention is a limited resource. Every minute spent scrolling is a minute not spent elsewhere. That doesn't make so- cial media inherently wasteful, but it does invite reflection. What if we treated our digital habits the way we treat our diets? Occasional indulgence is harmless, even enjoyable. But a constant stream of emotional junk food eventually affects how we feel. Reclaiming attention doesn't require leagues vacationed and which concerts were completely content with their lives, deleting every app. Sometimes it simply friends attended. Yet that knowledge they might spend less time browsing as- means pausing long enough to notice the doesn't necessarily create emotional pirational lifestyles or seeking validation difference between connection and com- closeness. through likes. pulsion. The result can feel like social prox- Others believe the issue is less inten- imity without genuine connection. tional and more structural. Engineers A final scroll A crowded digital room where every- optimize algorithms for engagement be- Close your phone for a moment and one is present, but not always truly en- cause that's what the system rewards. look around. The room is quieter than gaged. Emotional consequences become unin- the internet. Conversations are slower. tended side effects. The truth probably Emotions feel less amplified. Are we being engineered to feel lies somewhere in between. Real life rarely delivers the constant dissatisfied? Few designers set out to harm mental stimulation of a social feed. It moves This question often surfaces in de- health. Yet platforms optimized solely unevenly, sometimes awkwardly, some- bates about digital well-being. for engagement can shape emotional times beautifully. And perhaps that's the Social media platforms are, funda- habits in ways nobody fully anticipated. point. mentally, businesses. Their revenue de- The digital world is designed to cap- pends heavily on advertising, which in The human response: awareness ture our attention. But happiness, in its turn depends on attention. The longer and balance quieter form, rarely demands constant users remain on the platform, the more Despite these concerns, social media scrolling. valuable that attention becomes. itself isn't inherently harmful. For many It appears in small, unfiltered mo- In that environment, engagement be- people, it remains a powerful tool for ments. A conversation that lingers. A comes the ultimate metric. creativity, community and information. walk without notifications. A day that Content that provokes strong reac- Artists find audiences. Small busi- doesn't need to be posted. tions performs better. Outrage spreads nesses grow. Social movements gain Social media isn't stealing happiness quickly. Envy spreads quietly. Both keep visibility. Conversations about mental entirely. Yet if we're not careful, it can people scrolling. health that once felt taboo now reach convince us that happiness is always Some critics argue the system subtly millions. somewhere else. Just one more scroll benefits from dissatisfaction. If users The problem emerges when the digi- away. ■
MARCH 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 7

INSIGHT
Water & Language Rivers have nurtured civilizations,
droughts have shaped migrations and rain has determined whether com- When words munities thrive or struggle. Over time, these realities have seeped into the way people speak. In many ways, water doesn’t just sus- tain life. It shapes language itself. flow like rivers When rain needs many names
Consider the languages of northern Europe. In English alone, rain appears in a surprising variety of forms: drizzle, shower, downpour, cloudburst, sprinkle, It’s a small reminder that language, like water, flows mist, sleet. Each word paints a slightly different picture of the sky. through human history. It gathers meaning along the Move further north to Scotland and the vocabulary becomes even richer. way, carving paths through culture, memory and Scots reportedly has dozens of terms re- lated to rain, including dreich for dull, everyday life. By Aarav Sharma wet weather and smirr for a fine, persist- ent drizzle. These distinctions matter in a landscape where rain is almost a daily ow many words do you have for touches the earth. A relentless downpour companion. The language mirrors the rain? It sounds like a simple that floods fields. A sudden burst of weather's endless variations. Hquestion, yet it quietly reveals monsoon rain that transforms dry streets Across the Atlantic, in parts of the something profound about how people into rivers within minutes. Caribbean, rainfall also invites expres- live. In some languages, rain has only a Language, after all, is not just a sys- sive language. Islanders speak of handful of names. In others, it arrives in tem of grammar and vocabulary. It is a “squalls” that appear suddenly and va- dozens of shades and expressions, each reflection of human experience. And nish just as quickly. Farmers, fishermen describing a slightly different mood of water, in its many forms, has shaped that and sailors often rely on these nuanced the sky. A soft drizzle that barely experience for thousands of years. descriptions to understand what kind of

The number of words a culture has
for rain often reveals
how closely its people have listened
to the sky.”
weather they are facing. Meanwhile, in Japan, rain occupies a deeply poetic space in language. Words like kosame describe light rain, while ni- tions. waka-ame refers to a sudden shower. Even seasonal rains have names. They reflect a long history merous words describing different states of living closely with the sky. of dryness, thirst or drought. bedded within them. Words that describe specific winds, currents or rainfall pat- terns might vanish along with the com- munities that created them. 10 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MARCH 16 2026

PERSPECTIVE
Metaphor and Machine
or centuries, poetry has been one of the most intimate forms of Fhuman expression. A few care- fully chosen lines could capture grief, The changing longing, rebellion or love. Poems carried the rhythm of breath, the hesitation of thought and the emotional tremor of the person who wrote them. Yet today po- etry finds itself in an unexpected conver- sation with technology. soul of poetry Scroll through social media and po- etry appears everywhere. Short verses sit between travel photos, news updates and memes. Some are written by people. Others are generated by artificial intelli- In an age of algorithms, the most powerful lines may gence. Increasingly, it’s becoming har- der to tell the difference. still come from the fragile, complicated and beautifully Poetry remains a cultural treasure, imperfect human heart. By Anaya Gupta but in the digital age it also competes with algorithms that decide what we see, like and share. The question is no longer carried history, emotion and identity. The digital era has altered this whether machines can write poems. Consider the Persian ghazals of rhythm. They already do. Hafez, the haiku traditions of Japan, the Poetry now lives inside timelines and The deeper question is whether those romantic reflections of William Words- social feeds, surrounded by the restless poems can truly mean something. worth or the passionate lines of Pablo pace of online life. Neruda. Each poet wrote from a deeply Poetry before the algorithm personal place shaped by culture, mem- The rise of algorithmic poetry Long before screens and scrolling ory and experience. Today algorithms influence which feeds, poetry travelled through human Poetry was slow art. A poem could poems reach readers. voices. take months or even years to refine. Social media platforms study behav- Ancient poets recited verses in mar- Readers approached it with patience, re- iour. They track what users pause on, ketplaces and royal courts. In many cul- turning to the same lines again and share and react to. Content that gener- tures, poems were memorised and again, discovering new meanings over ates quick engagement rises to the top. passed down through generations. They time. Poetry, once a quiet and reflective form,
MARCH 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 11

“
Algorithms can imitate the rhythm
of poetry, but they cannot remember
the silence that gives a poem its
soul.”
The question of authenticity This raises an important question among writers and readers. If a poem moves us, does it matter who wrote it? Some argue that poetry should be judged purely by its effect. If a reader feels comfort or inspiration from lines written by a machine, perhaps the origin is irrelevant. Others strongly disagree. For them poetry is fundamentally a human act. It is not simply arranging beautiful words but sharing a fragment of consciousness. Imagine reading a powerful love poem and later discovering it was written by a program that has never felt love. The words might still sound con- vincing, but the emotional connection now competes with entertainment, news By analysing vast collections of writ- may shift. and viral trends. ing, AI systems learn patterns of lan- Readers often look for the voice be- This environment has produced a guage, rhythm and metaphor. With a hind the poem, the life that shaped its new style of poetry. It is often short, simple prompt such as “write a poem language. When machines enter that visually simple and emotionally imme- about loneliness,” the machine can pro- space, the boundaries of authorship diate. A few lines that can be read in duce verses in seconds. begin to blur. seconds and easily shared. Sometimes the results are surpris- Faster reading, shallower feeling? In some ways this shift has expanded ingly graceful. The imagery feels famil- Another change in the digital age is poetry’s audience. Young readers who iar. The structure resembles traditional the speed at which poetry is consumed. might never open a traditional poetry poetry. Occasionally, the lines even A poem that once invited quiet re- book now encounter verses online. carry emotional resonance. flection might now be read in a few sec- Some even begin writing their own Yet something subtle feels different. onds. A reader pauses briefly, taps a poems and sharing them with a global A machine has never experienced heart icon and moves on. community. heartbreak. It does not remember child- Poetry has become both more visible But the same system that amplifies hood streets or late-night conversations and more fleeting. poetry can also flatten it. with friends. It has never watched a Millions of people encounter verses Algorithms reward speed more than storm roll across the sea. every day through their screens. At the depth. A poem that demands reflection When AI writes about the ocean or same time, the constant flow of online may struggle in a culture built around the sky, it draws from patterns in data content makes it difficult to linger with a rapid scrolling. Virality often depends rather than from memory. poem. on simplicity rather than complexity. That difference matters. Are we reading more poetry but feel- And within this digital landscape, ar- For centuries poetry has been rooted ing it less deeply? tificial intelligence has quietly entered in lived experience. Even the most The answer is complicated. Some the creative process. imaginative poems grow out of emo- readers discover powerful poems online When machines write poems tional landscapes shaped by life. that stay with them for years. Others ex- Artificial intelligence can now gener- A machine, no matter how sophisti- perience poetry as a passing moment in ate poetry with remarkable fluency. cated, does not live inside that world. an endless digital stream.
12 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MARCH 16 2026

Poetry requires stillness. Digital cul- emotion and consciousness.
ture thrives on movement. “ That distinction may seem subtle, yet The tension between these two forces it defines the essence of poetry. shapes how poetry is experienced today. We may be reading Poetry is not merely about words. When technology supports poetry It is about presence. Despite these concerns, technology more poetry than The future of poetry has also opened new doors for poetry. The future will likely include both Online platforms allow poets from ever online, yet the poets and machines. different countries to share their work Artificial intelligence will continue to instantly. Writers who once struggled to real challenge is generate verses and assist writers. Algo- find publishers can now reach readers rithms will shape what readers en- directly. A poem written in one corner of whether we still counter online. Digital platforms will in- the world can travel globally within mi- pause long enough fluence how poetry spreads across the nutes. world. Digital archives have also made clas- to truly feel it.” Yet the core impulse behind poetry sical poetry more accessible than ever. may remain deeply human. Students can explore centuries of litera- People will still write poems after heartbreak. They will scribble lines in notebooks during quiet moments. They will search for words that capture feel- ings too complex for ordinary speech. Poetry has survived every techno- logical shift in history. Printing presses, radio and the internet all changed how poems travelled, but none erased the human desire to write them. Perhaps the real question is not whether machines can create poetry. It is whether humans will continue to need it. A quiet reflection Somewhere tonight, a young writer may still be staring at a blank page. No algorithm can fully predict what will happen next. A line will slowly ap- pear, shaped by memory, uncertainty and hope. It may not become viral. It may not even be perfect. But it will be unmistakably human. ture with a few clicks. Artificial intelligence itself can be- come a creative tool rather than a re- placement. Some poets experiment with AI to generate unexpected phrases or structures. Others use it as a starting point for new ideas. When used thoughtfully, technology can expand the creative possibilities of poetry. The challenge is ensuring that effi- ciency does not replace imagination. Can a machine feel a metaphor? At the heart of this debate lies a deeper philosophical question. Can a machine truly understand metaphor? Metaphor is not just a literary tech- nique. It is a bridge between emotion They can produce new metaphors and And perhaps that is what poetry has and language. When a poet writes, “my poetic images. But understanding them always been about. heart is a locked room,” the image in a human sense is another matter. In an age of algorithms, the most carries emotional weight shaped by per- Machines analyse language through powerful lines may still come from the sonal experience. patterns and probabilities. Humans un- fragile, complicated and beautifully im- Algorithms can imitate such patterns. derstand language through memory, perfect human heart. ■
MARCH 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 13

FEATURE STORY
Stage vs Screen
Theatre in the
The rise of binge culture Streaming has changed how people age of streaming Netflix and Amazon Prime Video trans- experience stories. Platforms such as formed entertainment from a scheduled event into an on-demand habit. Not long ago, viewers waited a week Streaming gives us convenience and endless choice. for the next episode of a television show. Today entire narratives arrive in a single Theatre gives us presence and connection. One fills drop. Watching five episodes in one evening is normal. In fact, many viewers our evenings; the other reminds us why stories matter feel slightly impatient when content is released slowly. in the first place. By Pooja Mehta This shift has shaped expectations about storytelling itself. Audiences are used to high production values, cine- hat is it about a dark theatre Prime Video, that question feels more matic visuals and constant narrative mo- hall that still feels different relevant than ever. Our screens offer mentum. Algorithms recommend what Wfrom watching a show at endless entertainment at the tap of a to watch next. The experience is per- home? Maybe it’s the hush that falls be- finger. Entire seasons appear overnight. sonal, curated and private. fore the curtain rises. Maybe it’s the Viewers binge-watch late into the night Compared with this, theatre can seem faint rustle of an audience settling into and pause whenever they like. slower, even demanding. It asks people their seats. Or perhaps it’s the simple Against this convenience, live theatre to leave their homes, travel to a venue awareness that whatever unfolds on might seem almost fragile. It cannot be and sit among strangers for two hours stage will happen only once in exactly paused. It cannot be replayed instantly. without checking their phones. that way. It demands time, presence and attention. So the obvious question emerges: In an age dominated by streaming Yet strangely, it continues to survive. why would audiences still choose live platforms like Netflix and Amazon And sometimes, it even thrives. theatre?
MARCH 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 15

The answer lies in something stream- series they might never have seen other- ing cannot easily replicate. wise. Presence. When theatres reopened, something had changed.
The power of being there Some people returned eagerly, almost “ A live performance carries an unpre- with relief. They missed the atmosphere, dictability that no screen can reproduce. the applause, the feeling of shared atten- Streaming may offer Actors breathe the same air as the au- tion. endless stories, but dience. A line delivered with unexpected Others hesitated. Habits had shifted. emotion can change the rhythm of a Convenience had won ground. theatre offers scene. A laugh spreads across the room Producers suddenly faced a new like a small wave. Even silence feels dif- question: how do you persuade au- something rarer: a ferent when hundreds of people share it. diences to return to a space that requires These moments are fragile. They ex- effort, when entertainment is available story that breathes ist only once. instantly at home? For theatre lovers, that ephemerality The answer, interestingly, has in- in the same room as is precisely the point. Watching Hamlet volved borrowing ideas from the digital on a laptop may offer convenience, but world itself. its audience.” watching it unfold on stage creates a communal experience. The story be- The rise of hybrid theatre comes something people feel together. In recent years, theatres have experi- stage production online, the next step Directors often describe theatre as a mented with hybrid models that com- may be seeing one in person. conversation between performers and bine stage performance with digital ac- Not everyone in the theatre com- audience. When spectators lean forward, cess. munity embraces this shift. Some worry actors sense it. When attention drifts, the Some productions are now filmed that filmed performances dilute the im- energy shifts. and streamed online after their live run. mediacy of the art form. Others see it as Streaming platforms offer extraordi- Others offer simultaneous broadcasts, a necessary evolution. nary storytelling. But they cannot rep- allowing viewers in different cities or After all, theatre has adapted before. licate that feedback loop of human pres- countries to watch the performance re- ence. motely. When screens inspire the stage Still, theatre’s survival has not been The National Theatre in London, for Ironically, the streaming boom has without challenges. example, launched a digital platform also influenced what appears on stage. where professionally recorded produc- Many modern theatre productions in- The shock of the pandemic tions are streamed globally. Broadway corporate cinematic elements such as When theatres around the world went shows have followed similar paths, re- projection screens, digital scenography dark during the pandemic, the fragility leasing filmed versions on streaming and multimedia storytelling. Lighting of the art form became painfully clear. services. design and sound technology now create Broadway shut down. London's West These initiatives serve multiple pur- immersive environments that rival vi- End went silent. Small community the- poses. sual experiences on screen. atres struggled to stay afloat. For They expand audiences beyond geo- Some plays even draw directly from months, stages stood empty while graphical limits. They allow students the world of digital culture. streaming platforms experienced record and theatre lovers to watch productions Stories about social media, online growth. that might otherwise be inaccessible. identity and virtual relationships in- Audiences grew comfortable watch- And perhaps most importantly, they in- creasingly appear in theatre scripts. ing stories from their living rooms. troduce new viewers to theatre itself. Playwrights explore the tension between Many discovered international films and For someone who first watches a digital life and physical presence, a
16 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MARCH 16 2026

greatest strength.
Can the stage survive? So can live theatre survive the age of streaming? The honest answer is yes, but not by ignoring the digital world. Theatres that thrive today tend to em- brace change while preserving the es- sence of the art form. They use technol- ogy creatively, reach audiences online and experiment with new storytelling formats. But at the center of it all remains the same simple act: people gathering in a room to watch other people tell a story. Streaming may dominate global en- tertainment. It offers scale, accessibility and astonishing variety.
“ The appeal of slowness equally powerful. Theatre offers something quieter, but There’s another reason theatre con-
tinues to matter. A shared moment that exists only once. It offers something increasingly rare: In a world of pause Streaming encourages constant con- buttons and slowness. A final thought sumption. One episode leads immedi- Perhaps the stage does not need to autoplay, theatre ately to another. Stories blur together. compete with the screen at all. They serve different emotional needs. Entertainment becomes background Streaming gives us convenience and remains one of the noise. endless choice. Theatre gives us pres- Theatre interrupts that rhythm. few places where When people sit in a theatre, they ence and connection. One fills our even- give their full attention to a single narra- ings; the other reminds us why stories stories unfold only tive. Phones are silenced. Distractions matter in the first place. fade. The experience becomes deliberate And sometimes, when the lights dim once, in real time.” rather than passive. and a voice speaks the first line of a For many younger audiences, this in- play, the world outside seems to pause tensity feels surprisingly refreshing. for a moment. theme audiences recognize instantly. Students who attend live perform- No buffering. No pause button. In a curious way, streaming culture ances often describe the experience as Just people, a stage and a story un- has become both competitor and inspira- more “real” than watching content on- folding in real time. tion. line. Not necessarily better, but different In an era of infinite content, that It pushes theatre artists to rethink in a way that feels meaningful. fleeting magic may be theatre’s greatest how stories are told. Perhaps that difference is theatre’s advantage. ■

EXPLORATION
Tech Tattoos The rise of bio-sensing tattoos
One of the most promising devel- opments is the bio-sensing tattoo. These The future sense. Instead, they are made with spe- are not traditional tattoos in the aesthetic cial inks that react to changes in the body. Imagine a tattoo that changes colour when your blood sugar rises. Or one that under the skin subtly shifts shade when you're dehy- drated. Researchers at institutions like MIT and Harvard have experimented with biosensitive inks that can detect varia- tions in glucose, sodium and pH levels Still, it’s hard not to feel a sense of curiosity about in the skin. Instead of pricking a finger to test blood sugar, a person could where this path leads. By Riya Verma simply glance at a small patch of tat- tooed skin. For people living with chronic con- hat if your tattoo could do tists, designers and engineers are now ditions such as diabetes, the idea is more than tell a story? What experimenting with “tech tattoos”, a new quietly revolutionary. Monitoring health Wif it could quietly monitor generation of body art that may blur the could become effortless, almost invisi- your health, unlock your phone, or even line between decoration and device. ble. The tattoo becomes a silent guard- change appearance through augmented It sounds futuristic. Yet in labo- ian under the skin. reality? ratories and design studios around the Of course, the technology is still For centuries, tattoos have been world, the idea is already taking shape. evolving. Accuracy, safety and long- deeply personal. Symbols of identity, re- And strangely enough, it feels less term durability remain questions scien- bellion, memory or belonging. But like science fiction and more like the tists are working through. But the princi- something interesting is happening at the natural evolution of wearable technol- ple is compelling. Our bodies already intersection of art and technology. Scien- ogy. hold enormous information. Tech tattoos
18 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MARCH 16 2026

the cultural dimension. Tattoos have long carried personal meaning and so- cial history. Some people may worry that turning them into gadgets could di- lute that symbolism. But technology has always reshaped how we express ourselves. Clothing once carried purely practical value be- fore becoming fashion. Phones started as communication tools before evolving into lifestyle devices. Tattoos may be entering a similar transformation. A glimpse of the future may simply make that information vis- ible.
Smart ink and the body as interface Another fascinating branch of inno- vation involves conductive or electronic tattoos. Unlike permanent ink, some of these designs are ultra-thin electronic circuits printed onto the skin, almost like temporary tattoos. These “electronic skin” systems can track heart rate, muscle movement or body temperature. Some prototypes can even transmit that data to smartphones. Athletes, for example, could monitor muscle fatigue during training. Patients recovering from surgery might wear such tattoos to track vital signs without bulky equipment. It’s a small shift, but a dragon. A tattoo of a bird could appear Perhaps the most interesting thing meaningful one. Medical monitoring be- to flap its wings. A memorial tattoo about tech tattoos is how quietly they comes lighter, more comfortable and might reveal a short video or message change our relationship with technology. less intrusive. when scanned. Wearable devices today still feel ex- The broader idea is that our skin may It’s a curious blend of physical per- ternal. We charge them, remove them, become an interface. manence and digital fluidity. The ink forget them on bedside tables. We already carry technology every- stays the same, but the experience Tech tattoos suggest something more where. Phones in our pockets, watches evolves through software. intimate. Technology that blends with on our wrists, earbuds in our ears. Tech For younger audiences raised in a the body rather than sitting on top of it. tattoos suggest a future where technol- world of filters, avatars and virtual Will everyone embrace that idea? ogy doesn’t sit on us but becomes part spaces, this kind of hybrid art feels sur- Probably not. of us. prisingly natural. Some people will always prefer tat- That thought excites some people. It The body becomes both canvas and toos that carry stories, memories or cul- unsettles others. portal. tural roots rather than circuits and sen- Both reactions are understandable. sors. And that’s perfectly fine. Tradition Between expression and and innovation often coexist in unex- Tattoos that come alive with surveillance pected ways. augmented reality Still, the rise of tech tattoos raises Still, it’s hard not to feel a sense of Then there is the playful side of tech questions that go beyond aesthetics. curiosity about where this path leads. tattoos. The kind that merges body art If tattoos begin collecting health data, The next time someone gets a tattoo, with augmented reality. who owns that information? If electronic it might not only express who they are. Some tattoo artists and digital de- tattoos connect to smartphones or medi- It might quietly monitor their heartbeat, signers are already creating tattoos that cal systems, how secure are they? And respond to their environment or come interact with smartphone apps. When how comfortable are people with tech- alive through a screen. viewed through a phone camera, the tat- nology that sits directly on their skin, For thousands of years, tattoos have too can trigger animation, music or a 3D potentially tracking their bodies around marked the human body as a canvas of visual effect on the screen. the clock? meaning. A simple geometric pattern on the Privacy experts have already begun Now, they may also become a win- arm might transform into a moving discussing these concerns. There’s also dow into the future. ■

IN-DEPTH
Power and Inequality Lohiya was not simply a socialist leader or a freedom fighter. He was a sharp critic of centralized power, a champion of linguistic and cultural diversity and Lohiya’s lens on hierarchies. More than five decades after an early voice against entrenched caste his death, his name travels easily through Indian politics, but the sub- stance of his thinking often does not. To understand why Lohiya still modern India matters, one must look beyond cere- monial remembrance and examine how his ideas shaped regional politics, social justice movements and the uneasy rela- tionship between ideology and power in Looking at Indian politics through a Lohiyaite contemporary India.
lens reveals patterns that might otherwise go The socialist who questioned power unnoticed. By Rajeev Khanna Lohiya belonged to that generation of leaders who were shaped by the freedom struggle yet remained deeply dissatisfied hat would Indian politics look acy. Yet, if one listens closely, some- with the direction independent India like if ideas mattered more thing feels missing. The debate around took. Unlike many of his contempo- Wthan slogans? It’s a question his ideas, the radical questions he raised raries, he refused to settle comfortably that quietly hangs in the air whenever about power, caste and inequality, rarely into the dominant political framework of the name of Ram Manohar Lohiya is in- makes it into mainstream political con- the time. voked in political speeches today. His versation. His socialism was not imported portrait appears at party offices, anniver- The birth anniversary of Ram Mano- wholesale from Europe. It was rooted in saries are marked with solemn tributes har Lohiya falls on March 23, a date that Indian realities. Lohiya argued that class and leaders across the ideological spec- marks the arrival of one of India’s most struggle alone could not explain inequal- trum claim some connection to his leg- restless and original political thinkers. ity in India. Caste, language, gender and
20 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MARCH 16 2026

“
In Indian politics today, Lohiya’s name travels
widely, but the
courage to debate his ideas rarely follows.”
that democracy must address social in- equality directly. In the decades following independ- ence, India’s political discourse largely revolved around development and na- tion building. Issues of caste discrim- ination and social exclusion were ac- knowledged but not always confronted with urgency. Lohiya took a different approach. He argued that political democracy without social equality would remain incom- plete. His advocacy for reservations for regional disparities were equally power- Kumar built their political careers within backward classes laid intellectual ful forces shaping society. networks shaped by Lohiyaite thought. groundwork for later policies aimed at He famously proposed what he called For instance, the rise of backward expanding representation in education the “Sapta Kranti” or Seven Rev- caste politics in northern India during and government jobs. olutions. 22 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MARCH 16 2026

wrapped in string or inserted into wooden holders, creating the earliest Before the Keyboard versions of what we now recognize as a pencil. By the eighteenth and nineteenth FEATURE centuries, pencil-making had become an There was industry. Among the companies that transformed it into a global craft was the German firm Faber-Castell, founded in 1761. Over time, it developed tech- niques for shaping wood casings, stan- dardizing graphite mixtures and produc- graphite ing pencils on a scale never seen before. Another legendary German brand, Staedtler, established in the nineteenth century, refined the manufacturing pro- cess further. Both companies helped turn the pencil from a simple writing stick Because sometimes progress doesn’t begin with into a carefully engineered tool. It may seem like a small evolution. complexity. Sometimes it begins with a question, a But that refinement mattered. Because when tools become reliable, ideas be- blank page and a pencil poised above it. By Ishita Desai come easier to capture.
hat if the most powerful tool behind modern civilization Wisn’t a supercomputer, but a simple stick of wood and graphite? It sounds almost absurd in an age of artifi- cial intelligence, quantum computing and digital design software. Yet for cen- turies, before engineers wrote code, be- fore architects opened CAD programs, before astronauts orbited Earth, some- one sat at a desk with a pencil and began sketching an idea. The pencil rarely appears in grand histories of innovation. It is too ordinary, too quiet. It doesn’t flash or beep or up- date itself overnight. And yet, from bridges and buildings to spacecraft and satellites, many of the world’s most transformative ideas were first born as faint graphite lines on paper. The modern world, in many ways, began with a pencil.
A discovery that changed writing The story of the pencil begins in the mid-sixteenth century in the English vil- lage of Borrowdale, where an unusually pure deposit of graphite was discovered beneath the ground. At first, people did- n’t even know what the material was. They simply noticed it left dark marks on paper and wood. Shepherds used pieces of graphite to mark their sheep. Craftsmen used it to sketch designs. Soon the material was
MARCH 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 23

“
Long before algorithms
and software shaped the modern world, a pencil quietly sketched
the first outlines of humanity’s biggest
ideas.”
The first draft of every invention Look closely at the history of engi- neering and architecture and you’ll no- tice a quiet pattern. Before blueprints. Before simula- tions. Before computer modeling. There was always a sketch. Leonardo da Vinci filled notebooks with pencil-like drawings of machines centuries before modern engineering ex- isted. In the nineteenth century, engi- neers designing railways and bridges re- lied on graphite lines to test their ideas. Architects drafted entire cities using pencils and rulers. Even today, many designers still begin with hand sketches. Something about the tactile movement of graphite across paper encourages experimenta- tion. Mistakes are easy to erase. Ideas open design software. The reason is sim- sitioning to specialized pressurized pens evolve organically. ple: drawing helps them see. designed for spaceflight. Still, the fact Digital tools are powerful, of course. And seeing, in architecture, is every- that pencils were once trusted tools But they often come later in the process. thing. aboard spacecraft says something pro- The first spark usually arrives as a rough found. drawing, a scribble, a quick outline of When NASA turned to pencils Even at the frontier of space explora- possibility. Even space exploration owes a quiet tion, humanity relied on graphite and In other words, the pencil is not just a debt to the humble pencil. wood. writing tool. It’s a thinking tool. In the early days of the American space program, engineers at NASA The analog tool behind digital Sketching the modern skyline faced a surprisingly practical problem: revolutions Walk through any major city and how do astronauts write in space? Ironically, many of the technologies you’ll see the legacy of pencil sketches Regular pens didn’t work well in mi- that define our digital world were first rising in steel and glass. crogravity because ink relies partly on imagined with pencils. Every skyscraper once began as a gravity to flow. Early astronauts there- Early computer engineers sketched simple drawing. Architects used pencils fore carried pencils for note-taking, circuit layouts by hand. Game designers to test proportions, angles and structural checklists and calculations. drafted characters and worlds on paper balance. Entire neighborhoods were The solution seemed perfect. Pencils before turning them into pixels. Film di- imagined this way, page by page. wrote reliably in zero gravity, required rectors storyboard scenes with pencil Frank Gehry, one of the world’s most no pressurized ink and were easy to use. drawings before cameras ever start rol- influential architects, famously begins But the story didn’t end there. ling. many projects with loose pencil Engineers eventually realized that Silicon Valley itself, the heart of the sketches. These rough drawings capture wooden pencils posed a risk. Graphite digital revolution, has a long tradition of movement and energy that rigid digital dust and wood fragments could float in whiteboards and sketchpads. Engineers tools sometimes struggle to replicate. the spacecraft cabin, potentially interfer- scribble diagrams and algorithms before Students in architecture schools ing with delicate electronics or even coding them into existence. around the world still spend hours prac- being inhaled by astronauts. It’s a reminder that innovation rarely ticing pencil sketching before they ever By the mid-1960s, NASA began tran- begins with a finished product. It begins
24 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MARCH 16 2026

notebooks.
And then “ there’s the emo- tional dimen- Every spacecraft, sion. A pencil carries a certain skyscraper and intimacy. It feels circuit board once personal in a way that screens existed as a fragile sometimes don’t. graphite line on a A child learn- ing to write their sheet of paper.” name. An architect sketching a They sit quietly on desks and in pencil dream building. cases, rarely attracting attention. A student Yet the pencil has shaped education, drafting the first engineering, architecture and art in ways lines of a poem. that few technologies ever have. Entire All of it be- industries have relied on it. Generations gins with graph- of students have learned with it. Some ite. of humanity’s greatest ideas began as faint graphite lines that could easily have been erased. In a world obsessed with the newest gadget, the pencil remains stubbornly simple. Maybe that’s the point. Because sometimes progress doesn’t begin with complexity. Sometimes it be- gins with a question, a blank page and a pencil poised above it. And somewhere, right now, someone is sketching the next idea that will change the world. ■
with an idea. And ideas are messy. A tool that grows with us The pencil, with its erasable graphite Another quiet virtue of the pencil is and forgiving simplicity, welcomes that its adaptability. Unlike ink, graphite can messiness. be erased. Ideas can evolve. Lines can be redrawn. Why pencils still matter in the That flexibility encourages creativity. digital age A pencil invites experimentation be- In classrooms across the world, chil- cause it forgives mistakes. The smudge dren still learn to write with pencils be- of graphite becomes part of the process fore they ever type on keyboards. rather than a permanent flaw. There’s a reason for that. Perhaps that’s why so many thinkers Writing by hand engages the brain keep pencils nearby even in our digital differently. Studies have shown that stu- age. They know that the earliest stages dents often retain information better of creativity require freedom, not preci- when they take notes with a pencil sion. rather than typing them. And few tools offer that freedom Artists value pencils for their subtle better than a pencil. shading and control. Designers use them to explore form and texture. Scientists A small object with a vast legacy jot quick equations or diagrams in lab It’s easy to overlook everyday tools.
MARCH 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 25

IN FOCUS
The Lonely Ritual
Whiskey feels poetic. Yet somehow whiskey,
more than any other drink, has become its literary companion. The relationship between whiskey and solitude isn’t accidental. It has been and the art of masculinity and the quiet rituals people shaped by storytelling, cultural ideas of build when they need a moment alone with their thoughts.
drinking alone Literature’s favourite companion Writers have long turned to whiskey when describing moments of introspec- tion. Think of the weary detective in crime fiction, the war veteran remem- bering things he’d rather forget, or the aging writer staring out of a rain-soaked Perhaps the reason whiskey and loneliness window. Whiskey works well in these scenes remain culturally linked is that both invite because it carries a certain emotional weight. Unlike champagne, which cele- contemplation. By Aditya Joshi brates, or beer, which suggests casual sociability, whiskey feels slower, more deliberate. In literature, the drink often becomes hy does a man sitting alone pears reflective, almost dignified. The a symbol rather than just a beverage. Er- with a glass of whiskey look room is dimly lit. The glass catches the nest Hemingway’s characters drink it Wthoughtful rather than trou- light. Somewhere between the first sip while confronting loss. Raymond Chan- bled? It’s a curious cultural image. In and the last, a private conversation dler’s detectives pour it after long nights films, novels and even advertisements, seems to unfold. chasing uncomfortable truths. Even in the solitary whiskey drinker often ap- But loneliness, in real life, rarely contemporary novels, the solitary glass
26 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MARCH 16 2026

still signals something deeper. A pause. A reckoning. Readers recognize the ritual. A per- son pours a drink, sits down and lets their mind wander. It’s less about intoxi- cation and more about reflection. The act becomes almost meditative. Of course, literature also shapes how we see the world. After encountering the image repeatedly, the solitary whiskey drinker starts to look less lonely and more philosophical. But that’s storytelling at work. The bar as a modern confessional Step into almost any quiet bar late in the evening and you’ll notice something interesting. Conversations tend to be- come unusually honest. Strangers share fragments of their lives. Bartenders listen patiently, some- times offering advice, sometimes simply nodding. In a way, the bar functions as a modern confessional. Whiskey often plays a central role in this setting. Perhaps because it’s usually sipped slowly, it encourages conver- sation rather than quick consumption. One drink lasts long enough for some- one to gather the courage to say what’s really on their mind. tions go? That said, solitude itself isn’t always Bartenders around the world will tell Sometimes, they end up in quiet rit- negative. Many people genuinely value you similar stories. The exhausted office uals. A drink after work. A solitary mo- moments of quiet reflection. A solitary worker talking about a failing relation- ment on the balcony. A glass poured drink at the end of a long week can feel ship. The traveler passing through with a slowly while replaying the day’s events. less like loneliness and more like a story no one back home would under- Psychologists have often pointed out pause in a noisy world. stand. The regular who sits quietly at the that alcohol can function as a socially The difference lies in intention. Are same corner stool every Friday night. acceptable emotional outlet. It creates a we escaping something or simply giving There’s something oddly comforting temporary space where reflection feels ourselves time to think? about these spaces. People may arrive permissible. The drink becomes a prop, alone, but they rarely feel completely in- almost an excuse to sit with feelings that A glass, a moment, a story visible. might otherwise remain unspoken. Perhaps the reason whiskey and lone- And sometimes, a quiet glass be- That doesn’t mean whiskey causes liness remain culturally linked is that comes the bridge between silence and loneliness. More often, it simply accom- both invite contemplation. confession. panies it. The drink slows us down. It asks to be sipped rather than rushed. And in that Masculinity, silence and the The thin line between reflection slow rhythm, thoughts surface that whiskey glass and isolation might otherwise stay buried beneath Whiskey’s connection with masculin- Still, the romantic image has its daily distractions. ity runs deep. limits. Sometimes those thoughts are heavy. For generations, popular culture has What literature often portrays as Sometimes they’re unexpectedly hope- portrayed the drink as rugged and thoughtful solitude can sometimes drift ful. strong. Western heroes, detectives, sol- into genuine isolation. A single drink to Either way, the image of the solitary diers and businessmen often reach for unwind may gradually turn into a whiskey drinker endures because it cap- whiskey rather than wine or cocktails. nightly habit. Reflection can become ru- tures a familiar human moment. A quiet The message is subtle but persistent. mination. room. A glass in hand. A mind wander- Whiskey is serious. Whiskey is mascu- Writers themselves have acknowl- ing through memories, questions and line. edged this tension. Many celebrated au- half-formed answers. But this symbolism also intersects thors who romanticized solitary drinking Not every story needs an audience. with another cultural expectation. Men also struggled with its consequences. Some are meant for the space between a are often discouraged from expressing Their work captured the atmosphere of person and their thoughts. vulnerability openly. those quiet moments but rarely the long And occasionally, a small amber So where do those unspoken emo- mornings that followed. glass. ■
